content.text:
Here's a summary of your Feb 24th work on the `security-sweep` branch:

---

## Feb 24 — What You Did

### Early afternoon: Repo bootstrapping (3:38–4:20 PM)
- Initial repo setup and committed the macOS app skeleton ported from `audiotake2`
- Initialized `beads` for issue tracking

### Evening: CI/CD + tooling (6:47–9:01 PM)
- Added full CI/CD pipeline, code quality checks (SwiftLint/SwiftFormat), and SPM package caching
- Fixed existing force unwrap issues
- Upgraded `actions/cache` to v5.0.3
- Migrated beads from Dolt server backend to JSONL-based storage (issues versioned in git)

### Evening: Security sweep (9:23–10:05 PM)
You identified 7 security issues via code review, filed them as beads tasks, then immediately fixed all of them:

| Beads Task | Fix |
|---|---|
| PABLO-D-96 | Replaced `DemoEncryptor` (hardcoded key) with production AES-256-GCM device-key encryption; renamed to `RecordingEncryptor`; deleted dead test file |
| PABLO-D-97 | Enforced HTTPS-only for all backend and auth URLs |
| PABLO-D-98 | Moved Firebase API key from `UserDefaults` → Keychain; also fixed it leaking as a URL query param |
| PABLO-D-100 | Passed Firebase key as `X-Goog-Api-Key` header instead of URL param |
| PABLO-D-101 | Added inline HTTPS validation errors in login and settings URL fields |
| PABLO-D-102 | Replaced hardcoded bundle ID strings across all services/ViewModels with `AppConstants` |

Also added:
- `AppConstants` + `URLValidator` shared utilities
- `AGPL-3` license and `SECURITY.md` policy
- App display name set to "Pablo", bundle ID corrected to `health.pablo.companion`

### Late evening: Lint cleanup (9:57–10:05 PM)
- Resolved all SwiftLint/SwiftFormat violations that the security changes introduced
- Fixed the `wrapMultilineStatementBraces` / `opening_brace` conflict (PABLO-D-98)
- Synced beads state and committed Claude skills + local settings

---

It was a focused security hardening session — you went from finding the issues to fixing all of them in a single sitting.

    11→{"id":"PABLO-D-100","title":"Pass Firebase API key as HTTP header instead of URL query parameter","description":"## Problem
TokenRefresher.swift embeds the Firebase API key directly in the URL query string:
```swift
let urlString = "https://securetoken.googleapis.com/v1/token?key=\(apiKey)"
```
Query parameters appear in server access logs, proxy logs, and browser history.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Firebase API key is passed as an HTTP header (e.g. `X-Goog-Api-Key` or per Firebase docs)
- URL no longer contains the key as a query parameter
- Verify Firebase token endpoint accepts key via header (check Firebase REST API docs)

    28→{"id":"PABLO-D-96","title":"Guard DemoEncryptor behind DEBUG flag and implement production key generation","description":"## Problem
DemoEncryptor.swift uses a hardcoded sequential key (0x01…0x20) committed to a public repo. Any recording 'encrypted' with this key is effectively plaintext — a HIPAA violation for a production app.

## Revised Approach (approved 2026-02-25)
Delete DemoEncryptor entirely. Replace with a single persistent device key stored in Keychain.

- Generate one AES-256-GCM key per device on first launch via SecRandomCopyBytes()
- Store in Keychain with kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly
- Use for all recordings on this device
- Decrypt in memory before upload (key never leaves device); backend receives plaintext over HTTPS
- Add 'Delete after upload' setting (defaults ON) in Settings

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] DemoEncryptor.swift deleted
- [ ] RecordingViewModel generates device key on first launch via SecRandomCopyBytes() if Keychain miss
- [ ] Key stored in Keychain with kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly
- [ ] Recordings encrypted at rest with AES-256-GCM using device key
- [ ] Recordings decrypted in memory before upload; key never transmitted
- [ ] Settings: 'After upload' toggle — 'Delete from device' (default ON) / 'Keep on device'
- [ ] RecordingViewModel encryption toggle removed from SettingsView in all builds (no Debug mode toggle)
- [ ] Xcode build zero warnings

## Files
- mac/PabloCompanion/Services/DemoEncryptor.swift (DELETE)
- mac/PabloCompanion/ViewModels/RecordingViewModel.swift
- mac/PabloCompanion/Views/SettingsView.swift
- mac/PabloCompanion/Services/KeychainManager.swift (add device key methods)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"kurtn@pablo.health","created_at":"2026-02-24T21:11:46.836534-05:00","created_by":"Kurt Niemi","updated_at":"2026-02-24T22:01:22.688735-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-24T22:01:22.688735-05:00","labels":["security","hipaa","critical"]}

    30→{"id":"PABLO-D-98","title":"Move Firebase API key from UserDefaults to Keychain; remove from Settings UI","description":"## Problem
The Firebase Web API key is stored in `@AppStorage("firebaseAPIKey")` (UserDefaults, plaintext) and exposed as an editable TextField in SettingsView. UserDefaults is unencrypted and readable by any process running as the same user.

The key is already fetched correctly from the Next.js `/api/config` endpoint on startup (`ContentView.swift:32-43`) — the issue is just that it's persisted to UserDefaults instead of Keychain, and unnecessarily surfaced in the Settings UI.

## Fix

On startup, after fetching from `/api/config`:
1. Write the key to Keychain (`KeychainManager`) instead of `@AppStorage`
2. On subsequent launches, read from Keychain first — only call `/api/config` if Keychain is empty

No backend changes needed. The Next.js `/api/config` endpoint already exists and works.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `@AppStorage("firebaseAPIKey")` removed from `AuthViewModel`
- [ ] Key written to Keychain after successful fetch from `/api/config`
- [ ] Key read from Keychain on launch; `/api/config` fetch only if Keychain miss
- [ ] Firebase API key TextField removed from `SettingsView`
- [ ] Key never appears in logs at any level
- [ ] Xcode build zero warnings

## Files
- `mac/PabloCompanion/ViewModels/AuthViewModel.swift`
- `mac/PabloCompanion/Views/ContentView.swift`
- `mac/PabloCompanion/Views/SettingsView.swift`
- `mac/PabloCompanion/Services/KeychainManager.swift`

    31→{"id":"PABLO-D-99","title":"Backend: add GET /api/config endpoint to deliver Firebase API key","description":"## Context

The macOS companion app (Pablo Companion) currently requires the Firebase Web API key to be manually entered by the therapist in Settings, where it is stored in UserDefaults (plaintext). This is a HIPAA security finding (PABLO-D-98). The fix requires the backend to deliver this key securely after authentication so the companion never needs to store it persistently.

**Important distinction:** The backend already uses the Firebase *Admin SDK* with Application Default Credentials (`firebase_init.py`) — this is a separate credential from the Firebase Web API key. The Web API key is what client apps use to call Firebase Auth REST endpoints (e.g. token refresh via `securetoken.googleapis.com`).

## What Needs to Be Built

Add a new authenticated endpoint:

```
GET /api/config
```

### Response Schema

```json
{
  "firebase_web_api_key": "AIzaSy..."
}
```

### Auth Requirement

Requires a valid Firebase ID token (`Authorization: Bearer <token>`). Use the existing `get_current_user_id` dependency — no MFA or BAA required, since this is called during the login flow before those checks complete.

### Implementation Notes

- Add `FIREBASE_WEB_API_KEY` to `Settings` in `backend/app/settings.py` (Pydantic `SecretStr` field, read from environment)
- Create `backend/app/routes/config.py` with the new router, following the existing pattern in `users.py` / `sessions.py`
- Register the router in `backend/app/main.py`
- The key is already present in the Firebase project — find it in the Firebase Console under Project Settings → General → Web API Key (same project used by `firebase_project_id`)
- Add the env var to Cloud Run / deployment secrets — do NOT commit the key

### Example Implementation

```python
# backend/app/routes/config.py
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends
from ..auth.service import get_current_user_id
from ..settings import get_settings

router = APIRouter(prefix="/api", tags=["config"])

@router.get("/config")
def get_client_config(
    _user_id: str = Depends(get_current_user_id),
) -> dict:
    """Return client configuration. Requires authentication."""
    settings = get_settings()
    return {
        "firebase_web_api_key": settings.firebase_web_api_key.get_secret_value(),
    }
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `GET /api/config` returns `{"firebase_web_api_key": "..."}` for authenticated requests
- [ ] Returns `401` for unauthenticated requests
- [ ] `FIREBASE_WEB_API_KEY` is read from environment — never hardcoded
- [ ] Env var added to Cloud Run secret config and documented in `DEVELOPMENT.md` / `.env.example`
- [ ] Endpoint appears in FastAPI `/docs`
- [ ] Unit test covers: authenticated success, unauthenticated 401
- [ ] No value logged at any log level

## Companion-Side Implementation

See PABLO-D-98. Once this endpoint ships, the companion fetches the key on session start, stores it in Keychain for the session lifetime only, and never writes it to UserDefaults.

{"id":"PABLO-D-100","title":"Pass Firebase API key as HTTP header instead of URL query parameter","description":"## Problem
TokenRefresher.swift embeds the Firebase API key directly in the URL query string:
```swift
let urlString = "https://securetoken.googleapis.com/v1/token?key=\(apiKey)"
```
Query parameters appear in server access logs, proxy logs, and browser history.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Firebase API key is passed as an HTTP header (e.g. `X-Goog-Api-Key` or per Firebase docs)
- URL no longer contains the key as a query parameter
- Verify Firebase token endpoint accepts key via header (check Firebase REST API docs)

{"id":"PABLO-D-96","title":"Guard DemoEncryptor behind DEBUG flag and implement production key generation","description":"## Problem
DemoEncryptor.swift uses a hardcoded sequential key (0x01…0x20) committed to a public repo. Any recording 'encrypted' with this key is effectively plaintext — a HIPAA violation for a production app.

## Revised Approach (approved 2026-02-25)
Delete DemoEncryptor entirely. Replace with a single persistent device key stored in Keychain.

- Generate one AES-256-GCM key per device on first launch via SecRandomCopyBytes()
- Store in Keychain with kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly
- Use for all recordings on this device
- Decrypt in memory before upload (key never leaves device); backend receives plaintext over HTTPS
- Add 'Delete after upload' setting (defaults ON) in Settings

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] DemoEncryptor.swift deleted
- [ ] RecordingViewModel generates device key on first launch via SecRandomCopyBytes() if Keychain miss
- [ ] Key stored in Keychain with kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly
- [ ] Recordings encrypted at rest with AES-256-GCM using device key
- [ ] Recordings decrypted in memory before upload; key never transmitted
- [ ] Settings: 'After upload' toggle — 'Delete from device' (default ON) / 'Keep on device'
- [ ] RecordingViewModel encryption toggle removed from SettingsView in all builds (no Debug mode toggle)
- [ ] Xcode build zero warnings

## Files
- mac/PabloCompanion/Services/DemoEncryptor.swift (DELETE)
- mac/PabloCompanion/ViewModels/RecordingViewModel.swift
- mac/PabloCompanion/Views/SettingsView.swift
- mac/PabloCompanion/Services/KeychainManager.swift (add device key methods)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"kurtn@pablo.health","created_at":"2026-02-24T21:11:46.836534-05:00","created_by":"Kurt Niemi","updated_at":"2026-02-24T22:01:22.688735-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-24T22:01:22.688735-05:00","labels":["security","hipaa","critical"]}

{"id":"PABLO-D-98","title":"Move Firebase API key from UserDefaults to Keychain; remove from Settings UI","description":"## Problem
The Firebase Web API key is stored in `@AppStorage("firebaseAPIKey")` (UserDefaults, plaintext) and exposed as an editable TextField in SettingsView. UserDefaults is unencrypted and readable by any process running as the same user.

The key is already fetched correctly from the Next.js `/api/config` endpoint on startup (`ContentView.swift:32-43`) — the issue is just that it's persisted to UserDefaults instead of Keychain, and unnecessarily surfaced in the Settings UI.

## Fix

On startup, after fetching from `/api/config`:
1. Write the key to Keychain (`KeychainManager`) instead of `@AppStorage`
2. On subsequent launches, read from Keychain first — only call `/api/config` if Keychain is empty

No backend changes needed. The Next.js `/api/config` endpoint already exists and works.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `@AppStorage("firebaseAPIKey")` removed from `AuthViewModel`
- [ ] Key written to Keychain after successful fetch from `/api/config`
- [ ] Key read from Keychain on launch; `/api/config` fetch only if Keychain miss
- [ ] Firebase API key TextField removed from `SettingsView`
- [ ] Key never appears in logs at any level
- [ ] Xcode build zero warnings

## Files
- `mac/PabloCompanion/ViewModels/AuthViewModel.swift`
- `mac/PabloCompanion/Views/ContentView.swift`
- `mac/PabloCompanion/Views/SettingsView.swift`
- `mac/PabloCompanion/Services/KeychainManager.swift`

{"id":"PABLO-D-99","title":"Backend: add GET /api/config endpoint to deliver Firebase API key","description":"## Context

The macOS companion app (Pablo Companion) currently requires the Firebase Web API key to be manually entered by the therapist in Settings, where it is stored in UserDefaults (plaintext). This is a HIPAA security finding (PABLO-D-98). The fix requires the backend to deliver this key securely after authentication so the companion never needs to store it persistently.

**Important distinction:** The backend already uses the Firebase *Admin SDK* with Application Default Credentials (`firebase_init.py`) — this is a separate credential from the Firebase Web API key. The Web API key is what client apps use to call Firebase Auth REST endpoints (e.g. token refresh via `securetoken.googleapis.com`).

## What Needs to Be Built

Add a new authenticated endpoint:

```
GET /api/config
```

### Response Schema

```json
{
  "firebase_web_api_key": "AIzaSy..."
}
```

### Auth Requirement

Requires a valid Firebase ID token (`Authorization: Bearer <token>`). Use the existing `get_current_user_id` dependency — no MFA or BAA required, since this is called during the login flow before those checks complete.

### Implementation Notes

- Add `FIREBASE_WEB_API_KEY` to `Settings` in `backend/app/settings.py` (Pydantic `SecretStr` field, read from environment)
- Create `backend/app/routes/config.py` with the new router, following the existing pattern in `users.py` / `sessions.py`
- Register the router in `backend/app/main.py`
- The key is already present in the Firebase project — find it in the Firebase Console under Project Settings → General → Web API Key (same project used by `firebase_project_id`)
- Add the env var to Cloud Run / deployment secrets — do NOT commit the key

### Example Implementation

```python
# backend/app/routes/config.py
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends
from ..auth.service import get_current_user_id
from ..settings import get_settings

router = APIRouter(prefix="/api", tags=["config"])

@router.get("/config")
def get_client_config(
    _user_id: str = Depends(get_current_user_id),
) -> dict:
    """Return client configuration. Requires authentication."""
    settings = get_settings()
    return {
        "firebase_web_api_key": settings.firebase_web_api_key.get_secret_value(),
    }
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `GET /api/config` returns `{"firebase_web_api_key": "..."}` for authenticated requests
- [ ] Returns `401` for unauthenticated requests
- [ ] `FIREBASE_WEB_API_KEY` is read from environment — never hardcoded
- [ ] Env var added to Cloud Run secret config and documented in `DEVELOPMENT.md` / `.env.example`
- [ ] Endpoint appears in FastAPI `/docs`
- [ ] Unit test covers: authenticated success, unauthenticated 401
- [ ] No value logged at any log level

## Companion-Side Implementation

See PABLO-D-98. Once this endpoint ships, the companion fetches the key on session start, stores it in Keychain for the session lifetime only, and never writes it to UserDefaults.

1→# Agent Instructions
     2→
     3→This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
     4→
     5→## Quick Reference
     6→
     7→```bash
     8→bd ready              # Find available work
     9→bd show <id>          # View issue details
    10→bd update <id> --status in_progress  # Claim work
    11→bd close <id>         # Complete work
    12→bd sync               # Sync with git
    13→```
    14→
    15→<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION -->
    16→## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
    17→
    18→**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
    19→
    20→### Why bd?
    21→
    22→- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
    23→- Git-friendly: Auto-syncs to JSONL for version control
    24→- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
    25→- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
    26→
    27→### Quick Start
    28→
    29→**Check for ready work:**
    30→
    31→```bash
    32→bd ready --json
    33→```
    34→
    35→**Create new issues:**
    36→
    37→```bash
    38→bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
    39→bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
    40→```
    41→
    42→**Claim and update:**
    43→
    44→```bash
    45→bd update bd-42 --status in_progress --json
    46→bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
    47→```
    48→
    49→**Complete work:**
    50→
    51→```bash
    52→bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
    53→```
    54→
    55→### Issue Types
    56→
    57→- `bug` - Something broken
    58→- `feature` - New functionality
    59→- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
    60→- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
    61→- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)
    62→
    63→### Priorities
    64→
    65→- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
    66→- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
    67→- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
    68→- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
    69→- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)
    70→
    71→### Workflow for AI Agents
    72→
    73→1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
    74→2. **Claim your task**: `bd update <id> --status in_progress`
    75→3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
    76→4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
    77→   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
    78→5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`
    79→
    80→### Auto-Sync
    81→
    82→bd automatically syncs with git:
    83→
    84→- Exports to `.beads/issues.jsonl` after changes (5s debounce)
    85→- Imports from JSONL when newer (e.g., after `git pull`)
    86→- No manual export/import needed!
    87→
    88→### Important Rules
    89→
    90→- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
    91→- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use

# Agent Instructions

This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.

## Quick Reference

```bash
bd ready              # Find available work
bd show <id>          # View issue details
bd update <id> --status in_progress  # Claim work
bd close <id>         # Complete work
bd sync               # Sync with git
```

<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION -->
## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)

**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.

### Why bd?

- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
- Git-friendly: Auto-syncs to JSONL for version control
- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion

### Quick Start

**Check for ready work:**

```bash
bd ready --json
```

**Create new issues:**

```bash
bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
```

**Claim and update:**

```bash
bd update bd-42 --status in_progress --json
bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
```

**Complete work:**

```bash
bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
```

### Issue Types

- `bug` - Something broken
- `feature` - New functionality
- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)

### Priorities

- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)

### Workflow for AI Agents

1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
2. **Claim your task**: `bd update <id> --status in_progress`
3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`

### Auto-Sync

bd automatically syncs with git:

- Exports to `.beads/issues.jsonl` after changes (5s debounce)
- Imports from JSONL when newer (e.g., after `git pull`)
- No manual export/import needed!

### Important Rules

- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
- ❌ Do NOT use external issue trackers
- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems

For more details, see README.md and docs/QUICKSTART.md.

<!-- END BEADS INTEGRATION -->

## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)

**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.

**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**

1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
   ```bash
   git pull --rebase
   bd sync
   git push
   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
   ```
5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session

**CRITICAL RULES:**
- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds

# Work Skill

Start working on a beads task - automatically creates a git worktree and updates task status.

## Usage

```
/work TASK-ID
/work search terms for task title
```

## Examples

```
/work THERAPY-lo7.1
/work modern frontend setup
/work backend models
```

## What This Does

Claude will:
- Search for the task (ask if multiple matches)
- Create a git worktree with descriptive name
- Update beads status to in_progress
- Give you the commands to switch to the worktree

## Instructions

When the user invokes this skill:

1. Parse the input to determine if it's a task ID or search term
2. If search term, use `bd search` to find matching tasks
3. If multiple matches, ask user to select one
4. Get task details with `bd show`
5. Create a descriptive worktree name from the task title
6. Create git worktree: git worktree add ../PROJECT-NAME-description -b TASK-ID-description
7. Symlink gitignored Claude settings into the new worktree so permissions carry over:
   ```bash
   # From the MAIN repo root (not the new worktree)
   MAIN_REPO="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
   WORKTREE_DIR="../PROJECT-NAME-description"
   # Ensure .claude dir exists in worktree
   mkdir -p "$WORKTREE_DIR/.claude"
   # Symlink settings.local.json (has auto-approve permissions, MCP config, plugins)
   ln -sf "$MAIN_REPO/.claude/settings.local.json" "$WORKTREE_DIR/.claude/settings.local.json"
   ```
8. Update beads: bd update TASK-ID --status=in_progress
9. Show user the cd command to switch to the worktree

## Directory Naming

- Use descriptive name from task title (kebab-case)
- Format: ../therapy-assistant-platform-descriptive-name
- Branch: TASK-ID-descriptive-name

Be friendly and interactive. Ask if anything is unclear.

---

# Epic Skill

Orchestrate an entire beads epic through a 4-phase workflow: analyze dependencies, design review, implement with HIPAA gates, and deliver a PR.

## Usage

```
/epic EPIC-ID
/epic search terms for epic title
```

## Examples

```
/epic THERAPY-z2q
/epic SOAP note improvements
/epic authentication overhaul
```

## What This Does

The orchestrator (Claude Code) acts as epic manager and coordinates a team of specialized agents through 4 phases:

1. **Analyze** — Planner maps dependency DAG into execution batches, flags HIPAA touchpoints
2. **Design Review** — Reads or creates design doc, presents for user approval
3. **Implement** — Coder + HIPAA reviewer work through batches with quality gates
4. **Deliver** — Creates PR with full traceability (design doc, tasks, HIPAA checklist)

Every phase has a **user approval gate**. No work proceeds without your sign-off.

## Instructions

When the user invokes this skill:

### Step 1: Resolve the Epic

1. Parse the input to determine if it's an epic ID or search term
2. If it looks like an ID (e.g., `THERAPY-z2q`), run `bd show <id>`
3. If it's a search term, run `bd search <term>` to find matching epics
4. If multiple matches, ask the user to select one
5. If no matches, suggest creating a new epic with `bd create`

### Step 2: Check for Resume

1. Read all child tasks of the epic via `bd show`
2. If some tasks are already closed, report: "Resuming epic — N of M tasks already completed"
3. Show which tasks remain open

### Step 3: Launch Epic Team Workflow

**CRITICAL**: Read `.claude/agents/epic-team.md` FIRST before doing anything else. That file defines the full 4-phase workflow, team structure, and agent roles. Execute it as written.

From this point forward, you are the **coordinator, not the doer**. Delegate all research and analysis to agents — do NOT perform deep codebase analysis, file reading, or scoping yourself. Your job is to:
- Create the team (`TeamCreate`)
- Spawn agents with clear prompts
- Present agent findings to the user at each gate
- Manage task status in beads

The 4 phases (all defined in `epic-team.md`):

- **Phase 1: Analyze** — Spawn **planner** (Explore type) to map execution batches, flag HIPAA touchpoints → present findings to user → **Gate 1**
- **Phase 2: Design Review** — Read/create design doc → present to user → **Gate 2**
- **Phase 3: Implement** — Create branch, spawn **coder** + **hipaa-reviewer** → work through batches → **Gate 3**
- **Phase 4: Deliver** — Push, create PR, close tasks, shut down team

### Key Behaviors

- **Orchestrator ≠ researcher**: Spawn agents for analysis. Your inline work should be limited to beads commands, team management, and gate presentations.
- **Session resilience**: If re-invoked on a partially-completed epic, skip closed tasks and resume
- **Scope discipline**: New work discovered during implementation becomes new beads tasks, not scope creep
- **HIPAA non-negotiable**: Any security concern blocks the PR
- **User is always in control**: Every gate requires explicit approval before proceeding

Here's a detailed retrospective on what went well with your prompting and AI agent use:

---

## What Went Well

### 1. Beads issues as AI-ready briefs

The security issues (PABLO-D-96 through PABLO-D-102) are the clearest example. Each one had:
- A specific **Problem** section with file paths and line numbers
- **Acceptance Criteria** as checkboxes (exact done conditions, not vague goals)
- A **Files** section that pre-answered "where do I look?"

PABLO-D-99 in particular is exceptional — it distinguishes Firebase Admin SDK credentials from Web API keys (a subtle point an agent might get wrong), includes the exact FastAPI implementation, the JSON schema, the env var name, and cross-references PABLO-D-98. An agent given that issue needs zero clarifying questions.

**Why it works:** You reduced hallucination surface area. The less an agent has to infer, the less it guesses wrong.

---

### 2. CLAUDE.md as a standing order document

Your `CLAUDE.md` covers architecture rules, design system, the Rust decision rule, Definition of Done, and git workflow — all in one place. This means every session starts with the same grounding. You never had to re-explain "use Keychain not UserDefaults" or "HTTPS only" mid-session — it was already law.

**Why it works:** System prompts age. A well-maintained project-level CLAUDE.md doesn't.

---

### 3. Identify → capture → implement, not identify + implement simultaneously

You did a code review pass, wrote all 7 security issues in beads, *then* started implementing. This is visible in the git timeline — the `chore: Add AGPL-3 license, security policy, and security issue backlog` commit at 9:23 PM came before any of the fix commits at 9:51+.

**Why it works:** Batching the analysis phase means the agent isn't making scope decisions mid-implementation. Issues are pre-scoped and pre-prioritized.

---

### 4. Atomic commits with beads IDs

Every fix is one commit, one issue ID. `fix: Move Firebase API key from UserDefaults to Keychain (PABLO-D-98)` — clean traceability in both directions. The git log is self-documenting.

**Why it works:** Granular commits let you bisect, revert, and review individual changes without untangling work. It also creates a clean audit trail, which matters for a HIPAA app.

---

### 5. Capturing discovered scope as new issues rather than scope creeping

When PABLO-D-96 implementation uncovered that the `deleteAfterUpload` toggle wasn't wired, you created PABLO-D-103 instead of just fixing it inline. That issue is sitting open right now, properly tracked, not lost.

**Why it works:** This is the discipline that keeps sessions from running long and finishing messy. The agent stays on task; discovered work gets filed for later.

---

### 6. Skills as encoded workflow

The `/work`, `/epic`, `/pr`, `/continue` skills mean the workflow isn't in your head — it's in the repo. The `work` skill even handles the worktree creation and settings.local.json symlink. The `epic` skill has explicit approval gates baked in.

**Why it works:** Workflow consistency across sessions. You don't rely on context to know "create worktree, update beads, land the plane" — it's automated and enforced.

---

### 7. AGENTS.md as a machine-readable quick reference

The "Landing the Plane" protocol in `AGENTS.md` — with mandatory steps and the explicit warning "Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds" — is the kind of hard constraint agents need. It prevents the common failure mode of an agent that considers a task done when code compiles but before it pushes.

---

### The meta-pattern

What ties all of this together: you've built infrastructure for the AI, not just instructions to it. CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, skills, beads — these are durable assets that compound across sessions. Yesterday's session was fast and clean in large part because of the scaffolding built before it.

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture. Let me also check the "big bang" commit at 18:47 vs the granular pattern later, and look at the PABLO-D-99 closure without any code in this repo.

Key issues:
1. **7 rework commits out of ~24 total** - roughly 30% of the commit count was cleanup/correction after the fact. The agent wrote code that didn't pass lint on first pass.

2. **The 18:47 "big bang" commit** - 680825c changed 35 files, 1922 insertions. This is the antithesis of the clean atomic commit pattern used later. 

That single commit bundled together CI/CD setup, SPM caching, force unwrap fixes, test suite, AudioCaptureKit migration, and SwiftLint configuration—at least five separate concerns that should've been isolated.

3. **Force unwrap fixed twice** - 680825c claimed to fix force unwraps but then 3d03e78 had to address more in KeychainManager after PABLO-D-96 implementation. The initial fix was incomplete.

4. **AppConstants bundle ID wrong** - Created with `com.pablo.companion`, then corrected to `health.pablo.companion` in a follow-up commit. The agent didn't get the identifier right the first time.

5. **struct→enum issue** - AppConstants and URLValidator were created as structs then changed to enums because of SwiftLint's `convenience_type` rule, which is documented in MEMORY.md.

6. **PABLO-D-99 closed with no code** - The backend task was marked closed at 9:45 PM with no reason given and no actual implementation in this repo.

7. **Rapid lint cleanup sequence** - Seven commits in three minutes around 9:58-10:01 PM were all rework, suggesting a batch of security fixes got written then failed linting checks.

8. **680825c is a massive omnibus commit** - 35 files changed in one commit, which breaks the atomic commit pattern.

I should also check whether PABLO-D-96 has the same issue of separating refactoring work from the actual implementation.
